title: Multiscale Vectorial Determination of Magnetic Order Parameters using Electron Magnetic Linear Dichroism
authors: Jan Hajduček, Jáchym Štindl, Ján Rusz, Vojtěch Uhlíř
abstract: We demonstrate electron magnetic linear dichroism as a quantitative probe of vectorial magnetic order parameters with nanometer resolution in transmission electron microscopy. Explicit inclusion of vectorial core-level exchange splitting into mixed dynamic form factor simulations accounting for dynamical diffraction enables direct reconstruction of the magnetic spin axis from momentum-resolved electron energy-loss spectra. The resulting dichroic signal is intrinsically separable from nonmagnetic anisotropy, exhibits a well-defined dependence on the Néel vector or magnetization orientation, and remains robust down to the atomic scale. Applied to the collinear antiferromagnetic and ferromagnetic phase of cubic FeRh, this approach allows quantitative real-space mapping of the magnetic vector. These results open a pathway to nanoscale spectroscopy and imaging of antiferromagnets and altermagnets where the generalized approach to electron dichroism provides direct access to different magnetic order parameters.
summary: This paper develops a method to use electron magnetic linear dichroism to map the direction of magnetic spins in antiferromagnets at the nanoscale. By using advanced simulations, the authors show that this technique can distinguish magnetic order from crystal anisotropy, providing a powerful tool for studying new magnetic phases like altermagnets.
main_problem: The challenge of nanoscale vectorial reconstruction of magnetic order parameters, particularly in compensated magnetic phases like antiferromagnets and altermagnets where net magnetization vanishes.
main_result: Demonstration of Electron Magnetic Linear Dichroism (EMLD) as a quantitative, robust, and scalable probe for determining the magnetic spin axis with nanometer to atomic resolution.
method: A unified vectorial simulation framework combining DFT-derived transition matrix elements, Mixed Dynamic Form Factor (MDFF) formalism, and dynamical diffraction theory.
model_or_system: The study focuses on the metamagnetic alloy FeRh, examining both its antiferromagnetic (AF) and ferromagnetic (FM) phases using Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM) and EELS.
key_observables: Electron Magnetic Linear Dichroism (EMLD), Electron Magnetic Circular Dichroism (EMCD), and Electron Natural Linear Dichroism (ENLD) signals.
important_parameters: Néel vector orientation (polar and azimuthal angles), momentum transfer (q), exchange-splitting energy, and probe convergence angle.
main_assumptions: The model assumes the absence of valence spin-orbit coupling for the simplified diagonal operator representation and utilizes the dipole approximation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows scattering geometry and simulated EELS spectra for FM/AF phases; Figure 2 illustrates EMLD intensity maps and dependence on the Neel vector; Figure 3 details the reconstruction scheme and angular dependence; Figure 4 shows EMLD maps as a function of beam tilt.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of magnetic reconstruction, presents a unified vectorial simulation framework, demonstrates the separation of dichroic signals, applies the method to FeRh, and evaluates the scalability and robustness of the EMLD probe.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Electron Magnetic Dichroism in TEM. (a) Schematic of the EMD scattering geometry with incident electron beam along ⃗ki, slightly tilted from ZA direction, undergoing elastic and inelastic scattering in FeRh. Elastic scattering forms an electron diffraction pattern (ED) with indicated Bragg spots. Energy-filtered intensities IΩ(E) are collected at symmetry- related aperture positions Ω. Spectral differences are obtained by comparing signals from Ω∥and Ω⊥for EMLD/ENLD, and from Ω+ and Ω−for EMCD, which isolates the corresponding dichroic components. Simulated EELS spectra at the Fe L3,2 edges, with color-coded jz-resolved transitions and the integrated spectrum shown in black. Panels...

FIG. 2. Linear dichroic signal dependence on AF spin axis. (a) Schematic of the scattering geometry with indicated Néel vector orientations. (b) Integrated ELD intensity maps in the diffraction plane normalized by the original spectral intensity for a given Néel vector orientation. The signal con- tains both EMLD and ENLD contributions, where the latter acts as a spin-independent background. The signal is evalu- ated at each ⃗q position at the Fe L3 edge in the momentum range of (−0.6, +0.6)G from the (000) Bragg spot.

FIG. 3. EMLD-based vectorial reconstruction of the AF spin axis. (a) Reconstruction scheme for an unknown Néel-vector orientation. EMLD spectra acquired at selected aperture positions progressively constrain the possible spin-axis directions on the orientation sphere, yielding two symmetry-equivalent Néel-vector solutions. (b-d) Simulated EMLD spectral profiles (insets) and corresponding integrated dichroic intensity as a function of θ ⃗ N and φ ⃗ N for representative aperture config- urations. The plots represent the full angular dependence of all possible EMLD responses rather than a single experimental measurement. The dichroic intensities follow a sin2-like harmonic dependence with...

FIG. 4. Crystal-orientation dependence of the EMLD signal in FeRh. Schematic experimental geometries used to probe the (a) in-plane and (b) out-of-plane components of EMLD. Calculated EMLD signal maps as a function of beam tilt magnitude and azimuth with respect to the [001] zone axis. Color scale encodes the sign and magnitude of the EMLD signal for each incident-beam direction and corre- sponding aperture configuration. Qualitatively similar beam- tilt dependencies of EMLD can be obtained for the FM case.
